office_does: One of 49 state senators; writes and votes on state law, sets state budgets, and confirms gubernatorial appointments for a four-year term.
race_blurb: Both candidates advance to the general regardless of the primary result. The Democratic incumbent, a committee chair, faces a Republican Covington city councilmember; both center affordability, housing supply, and public safety, differing mainly on means.

candidates.summary: Incumbent senator (2022-present, previously 2007-2011) and chair of the Senate Business, Trade & Economic Development Committee; describes herself as the first Native American woman elected to the Washington State Senate.
highlights: Top stated priority is 'making Washington affordable for everyone': housing supply plus protecting renters from 'predatory price gouging' [S1]
Cites childcare-access expansion, senior property-tax relief, and infrastructure funding for Highway 18 and SR 516 [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the mayors of Auburn, Covington, and Kent, County Executive Zahilay, and the WA State Labor Council [S1]

experience.basis: Incumbent senator with a committee chairmanship and prior Senate service (2007-2011), versus a municipal-level challenger.

housing.basis: Pairs increased housing supply with renter protections against 'predatory price gouging.'

spending.basis: Emphasizes state investment: childcare expansion, a robust social safety net, and infrastructure funding she cites securing.

candidates.summary: Covington city councilmember since 2019 and Ukrainian immigrant challenging the incumbent from a tax-opposition and spending-restraint platform.
highlights: Opposes recent B&O and gas-tax increases and an income tax; says the state has 'a spending problem rather than a revenue shortage' [S2]
Favors housing supply through permitting reform and market incentives rather than state mandates [S2]
On the council: launched the 'Clean Team' program, supported full police funding, and helped pioneer a community resource officer program [S1]

experience.basis: Challenger with municipal council experience since 2019 but no state legislative record.

taxes.basis: Opposes recent B&O and gas-tax increases and an income tax; frames the state as having a spending problem, not a revenue shortage.

spending.basis: Platform stresses accountability and spending restraint over new state programs.

housing.basis: Favors increasing supply through permitting reform and market incentives rather than state mandates.

safety.basis: Supports fully funded law enforcement and more officers, paired with addiction-treatment resources and a community resource officer program.

local-control.basis: Opposes state housing mandates in favor of local permitting reform and incentives.
